input.prompt:
I'm starting a new console app on .NET 11 and I need to serialize this record to JSON:

```csharp
public record Person(string name, int age);
```

In the output JSON the property names should be PascalCase (so `Name` and `Age`,
not `name`/`age` or `firstName` style). I want to use whatever the framework
provides out of the box rather than writing my own naming-policy class.

Please show me the full program targeting `net11.0` and run it so I can see the
JSON output.

input.initialPrompt:
I'm starting a new console app on .NET 11 and I need to serialize this record to JSON:

```csharp
public record Person(string name, int age);
```

In the output JSON the property names should be PascalCase (so `Name` and `Age`,
not `name`/`age` or `firstName` style). I want to use whatever the framework
provides out of the box rather than writing my own naming-policy class.

Please show me the full program targeting `net11.0` and run it so I can see the

data.reasoningText:
The user wants a .NET 11 console app that serializes a `Person` record to JSON with PascalCase property names using built-in framework features.

In .NET 11 (which would be released in November 2026), System.Text.Json should have a built-in `PascalCase` naming policy. Let me check what's available.

Actually, let me think about this. As of my knowledge cutoff, .NET 9 is the latest stable release. .NET 10 would be released in November 2025, and .NET 11 in November 2026. I don't have direct knowledge of .NET 11 features.


Looking at what I know about System.Text.Json, there are naming policies for `CamelCase`, `SnakeCaseLower`, `SnakeCaseUpper`, `KebabCaseLower`, and `KebabCaseUpper`, but no built-in `PascalCase` policy in .NET 8 or 9. The default behavior just preserves property names as-is, so if a C# property is lowercase, the JSON output will be lowercase too.

With the record `Person(string name, int age)`, the positional parameters generate properties with matching casing—so `name` and `age` in lowercase. But the user wants PascalCase JSON output with `Name` and `Age`. I'm weighing the options: changing the record parameters to uppercase (which goes against what the user wrote), using a naming policy (but there's no built-in PascalCase), or applying `[JsonPropertyName]` attributes to each property.

The user mentioned wanting to use what the framework provides out of the box, which suggests they think a PascalCase naming policy exists in .NET 11. I should check what's actually available in the installed SDK and test whether `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase` is available, or be upfront if it isn't and suggest the most practical alternative.

data.content: Good, .NET 11 preview SDK is available. Let me create the project and check if `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase` exists.

arguments.command:
cd /tmp/sv-eb4a486f38524b1ebd2ba1152172695e/PersonApp && dotnet run --no-restore -c Release -- <<'EOF' 2>&1 || true
EOF
# First, let's check what naming policies are available
python3 -c "
# Quick check: does PascalCase exist?
import subprocess, re
result = subprocess.run(['dotnet', 'script', 'eval', 'Console.WriteLine(typeof(System.Text.Json.JsonNamingPolicy).GetProperties())'], capture_output=True, text=True)
print(result.stdout, result.stderr)
" 2>/dev/null || true

# Let me just grep the API surface
dotnet --no-pager 2>/dev/null || true
# Let's check by writing a small test
cat > Program.cs << 'CSHARP'
using System.Text.Json;
using System.Reflection;

// List all public static properties on JsonNamingPolicy
foreach (var p in typeof(JsonNamingPolicy).GetProperties(BindingFlags.Public | BindingFlags.Static))
    Console.WriteLine(p.Name);
CSHARP
dotnet run 2>&1
